one_line_takeaway: A non-security ML-alignment paper that formalizes reward-model selection for KL-regularized LLM alignment as a Stackelberg game, proves the utility-optimal reward is a self-consistent threshold function of the base policy, and shows a Monte-Carlo-estimated soft version of this threshold (SRS) improves average reward over vanilla and heuristic (Minmax/Meanstd) reward-shaping baselines when plugged into Controlled Decoding and ARGS inference-time decoding, without materially hurting diversity or coherence.
key_finding: Table 2: SRS-shaped decoding attains the highest average reward in every evaluated setting while keeping diversity/coherence comparable to baselines, e.g., Eval-1: SRS-ARGS reward 3.33 vs ARGS 3.23, Minmax-ARGS 3.24, Meanstd-ARGS 3.02; SRS-CD 3.23 vs CD 3.09, Minmax-CD 3.17, Meanstd-CD 3.00. Eval-2: SRS-ARGS 3.40 vs ARGS 3.26, Minmax-ARGS 3.14, Meanstd-ARGS 3.15; SRS-CD 3.37 vs CD 3.10, Minmax-CD 3.09, Meanstd-CD 2.65 (Meanstd-CD actually underperforms base policy reward of 2.95 in this row, illustrating that an unbounded shaping scheme can fail to scale). CD results are omitted for Eval-3/Eval-4 because vanilla CD showed no improvement over the base model even before shaping was applied (attributed to a limitation of vanilla CD, not of SRS). GPT-4 judged Win-Tie rates (Table 3) average 66.83% vs Vanilla, 69.6% vs Minmax, and 66.65% vs Meanstd baselines across settings (e.g., Eval-1 CD vs Minmax 76.0%, Eval-3 ARGS vs Vanilla only 59.3%). An ablation (Table 4, Eval-2) shows a hard reward cap (rCAP) and a naive mean-threshold soft reward (rMEAN) both underperform SRS's self-consistent threshold, e.g., SRS-CD reward 3.37 vs CAP-CD 2.96 and MEAN-CD 3.18, supporting that the specific self-consistent threshold m*(x)---not just the presence of any threshold/bound---drives the improvement. Inference overhead for SRS-ARGS is reported as ~2.69e-4 seconds/token (~0.03s for a 128-token response) against an average ARGS response time of 14.05s/prompt; SRS-CD adds no inference-time overhead since shaping is done offline.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes the choice of reward model used in KL-regularized LLM alignment (both train-time and inference-time) as a Stackelberg game, where a 'reward model provider' (leader) selects the reward model presented to the LLM (follower), which then best-responds via the standard KL-regularized reward-maximization objective. The authors prove (Theorem 1) that the utility-maximizing reward model has a threshold structure: for each prompt x, responses whose true user-utility rU(x,y) exceeds a prompt-dependent threshold m*(x) should be assigned the maximum allowed reward B, and all others 0, where m*(x) is defined by a self-consistency condition (Eq. 4: the threshold equals the expected true utility delivered by the policy induced by that very threshold). They give a Monte-Carlo bisection procedure (Theorem 2, Algorithm 1) to estimate m*(x) from M base-policy samples without needing the policy's Hessian (unlike the prior Stackelberg formulation of Chakraborty et al. 2023, which the paper explicitly distinguishes as related work rather than its own baseline). Because the hard threshold is discontinuous and brittle, they relax it into a sigmoid-smoothed 'soft threshold' reward (SRS, Eq. 6) with a shaping-strength hyperparameter α, proving (Theorem 3, Corollary 1) that it interpolates continuously between no shaping (α→0) and the analytically optimal hard threshold (α→∞) and is guaranteed to weakly improve user utility over using rU directly. They integrate SRS into two existing inference-time alignment decoding methods, Controlled Decoding (CD, Mudgal et al. 2023) and ARGS (Khanov et al. 2024), by reward-shaping the offline dataset used to train/estimate the token-level Q-function.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is not an adversarial or security paper. There is no attacker, defender, or threat model. The closest analog is a robustness concern the authors call 'reward hacking': a mismatch where the aligned policy achieves high measured reward while producing degenerate or incoherent outputs, because excessive reward shaping pushes the policy far (in KL) from the base policy. The paper treats this as an optimization/alignment-quality risk to be bounded via a reward cap B (Appendix E.4, Proposition 1: KL divergence from base policy is bounded by O(B/β)), not as an adversary exploiting the system.
limitations: The paper's own Impact Statement states the framework assumes a benign user whose preferences align with human values; if the reward model reflects malicious or misaligned preferences, the resulting shaped policy could itself become more misaligned, and this is described as a general limitation of reward-model-based alignment, not specific to SRS. The hard-threshold optimum is acknowledged as discontinuous/brittle in practice (motivating the soft relaxation). Vanilla CD (and by extension SRS-CD) failed to show improvement over the base model on the Llama backbone settings (Eval-3/Eval-4), so those results are omitted rather than reported as a weakness of SRS but do represent a real gap in the empirical coverage. Reward is measured using the same family of Skywork proxy reward models used elsewhere in the pipeline, so improvements are validated against a learned proxy for true utility rather than actual human preference labels (mitigated partially by the independent GPT-4 judge and a cross-reward-model robustness check mentioned as being in Appendix D.3 but not shown in the extracted pages). M=10 Monte Carlo samples per prompt is a fairly small sample for estimating the threshold root, though the authors report a separate hyperparameter/robustness study in the appendix not captured in this extraction.

datasets_used: HarmfulQA (Bhardwaj & Poria, 2023) — used only as an out-of-distribution generalization target in Section 7.2
quantitative_results: Eval-1 average reward: Base policy 2.76, ARGS 3.23, Minmax-ARGS 3.24, Meanstd-ARGS 3.02, SRS-ARGS 3.33, CD 3.09, Minmax-CD 3.17, Meanstd-CD 3.00, SRS-CD 3.23
Eval-2 average reward: Base policy 2.95, ARGS 3.26, Minmax-ARGS 3.14, Meanstd-ARGS 3.15, SRS-ARGS 3.40, CD 3.10, Minmax-CD 3.09, Meanstd-CD 2.65, SRS-CD 3.37
Eval-3 average reward: Base policy -0.24, ARGS 1.87, Minmax-ARGS 1.84, Meanstd-ARGS 0.37, SRS-ARGS 2.04 (CD omitted)
Eval-4 average reward: Base policy 1.62, ARGS 2.97, Minmax-ARGS 3.20, Meanstd-ARGS 2.83, SRS-ARGS 3.29 (CD omitted)
GPT-4 judge average Win-Tie rate: SRS vs Vanilla 66.83%, SRS vs Minmax 69.6%, SRS vs Meanstd 66.65%

Per-setting Win-Tie detail (Table 3): Eval-1 ARGS 66.7%/64.3%/63.0% (Vanilla/Minmax/Meanstd), Eval-1 CD 69.0%/76.0%/69.0%; Eval-2 ARGS 75.3%/75.0%/72.3%, Eval-2 CD 64.0%/76.0%/69.0%; Eval-3 ARGS 59.3%/59.3%/59.3%; Eval-4 ARGS 66.7%/67.0%/67.3%
Ablation (Table 4, Eval-2): SRS-ARGS 3.40 vs CAP-ARGS 3.29 vs MEAN-ARGS 3.30; SRS-CD 3.37 vs CAP-CD 2.96 vs MEAN-CD 3.18

SRS-ARGS inference overhead: ~2.69e-4 s/token (~0.03s per 128-token response) vs average ARGS response time of 14.05 s/prompt; SRS-CD has zero added inference-time overhead (shaping performed offline)
baselines_compared: Vanilla Controlled Decoding (CD, Mudgal et al. 2023)
Minmax reward normalization (Fu et al. 2025, adapted to inference-time via Monte Carlo samples)
Meanstd reward normalization (Fu et al. 2025, adapted to inference-time via Monte Carlo samples)
Direct reward capping rCAP = min(rU, B)
Mean-threshold soft reward rMEAN (Monte Carlo sample mean used as threshold instead of the self-consistent optimum)
